skill.name: aeon
skill.description: Use Aeon for time series machine learning workflows, including classification, regression, clustering, forecasting, anomaly detection, segmentation, similarity search, transformations, distances, datasets, and benchmarking.
skill.summary: Guide time series machine learning with Aeon and scikit-learn compatible workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found the skill is markdown documentation with Python examples and API catalogs. The command execution, weak crypto, dynamic import, and combined critical heuristic findings are false positives caused by inline code, fenced examples, algorithm names, and public documentation links. Residual risk is low because the examples recommend installing Aeon and may load public datasets or benchmark results from external sources.

low_findings.title: External Dependency and Dataset Access Examples
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install the Aeon package and includes examples that load benchmark datasets or published results. This is legitimate for a data science skill, but users should understand that package installation and dataset loading can contact external package or data sources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show installation and dataset-loading examples with clear legitimate intent. The risk is limited to expected dependency and public data access, not hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Local Dataset Read and Write Examples
low_findings.description: The documentation shows examples for loading and writing local time series files. These are normal Aeon dataset workflows, but users should apply them only to trusted paths and data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit user-facing examples, not hidden behavior. They are low risk because no automatic execution or path manipulation is present in the skill.

low_findings.title: GPU Environment Variable Example
low_findings.description: The skill includes an example setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to select a GPU. This is a common machine learning configuration pattern and does not access secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is limited to CUDA device selection in a fenced Python example. No credential variables or external transmission are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Inline Code Misclassified as Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer treated many markdown inline API names and fenced Python examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found API documentation and example code, not executable shell backticks controlled by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown code formatting and Python examples. There is no Ruby code, shell interpolation, or automatic command execution path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python Grouped Imports Misclassified as Dynamic Import
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import findings point to normal Python import statements spread across multiple lines in documentation examples. They do not use JavaScript dynamic import expressions or load attacker-controlled modules.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a static Python import in a fenced markdown example. No runtime dynamic import expression or user-controlled module path is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Algorithm Name False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings appear to be triggered by ordinary time series terms and estimator names such as ShapeletTransformClassifier and dataset descriptions. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage was found in the reviewed locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text discusses time series methods and skill metadata, not cryptographic primitives. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, encryption, or password handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical combination finding combined code examples, public URLs, and a CUDA environment setting. Review found no credential access, no obfuscation, no prompt injection attempt, and no hidden network exfiltration intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are public documentation links, benign ML configuration, and benchmark result examples. No evidence found for credential collection, malicious network use, or obfuscated behavior.

content.value_statement: Time series projects require algorithms that handle temporal order, alignment, seasonality, and sequence-specific evaluation. This skill guides Aeon workflows for modeling, feature extraction, benchmarking, and model selection.
actual_capabilities: Select Aeon algorithms for classification, regression, clustering, forecasting, anomaly detection, segmentation, and similarity search.
Explain data format expectations for Aeon collections and series workflows.
Build scikit-learn compatible pipelines with Aeon transformers and estimators.
Compare temporal distance metrics such as DTW, ERP, EDR, LCSS, TWE, and MSM.
Use benchmark datasets, standard splits, published results, and task-specific metrics.
Recommend preprocessing, feature extraction, and validation practices for temporal data.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a running Aeon environment.
It assumes users can install Aeon and required Python dependencies separately.
Some examples load public datasets or benchmark results from external sources.
Deep learning workflows may require TensorFlow, compatible hardware, and careful tuning.

use_cases.title: Classify sensor recordings
use_cases.description: Choose classifiers, transformations, and validation methods for labeled temporal sensor data.

use_cases.title: Detect operational anomalies
use_cases.description: Select anomaly detection, segmentation, and visualization approaches for monitoring time-indexed signals.

use_cases.title: Benchmark forecasting methods
use_cases.description: Use standard datasets, metrics, and statistical tests to compare Aeon estimators reproducibly.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a starter model
prompt_templates.prompt: I have [describe time series shape, labels, and goal]. Recommend a simple Aeon workflow and explain the data format I need.

prompt_templates.title: Build a training pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Aeon pipeline for [classification, regression, clustering, or forecasting]. Include preprocessing, estimator choice, validation, and metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Compare algorithms
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Aeon algorithms for [task] on [dataset type]. Include baseline methods, stronger candidates, runtime tradeoffs, and evaluation metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose model results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these time series modeling results: [summarize metrics and data issues]. Identify likely causes and recommend Aeon adjustments.

output_examples.input: I need fast classification for many equal-length univariate sensor traces.
output: Start with MiniRocketClassifier because it is fast and strong across many datasets.
Normalize the collection and keep the train/test split fixed for comparison.
Use a simple distance-based classifier as a baseline before testing heavier ensembles.

output_examples.input: I need to find change points in a long production signal.
output: Try FLUSSSegmenter for speed or ClaSPSegmenter when pattern changes are important.
Smooth or normalize the signal before segmentation if noise dominates the boundaries.
Validate detected change points with plots and domain-specific tolerances.

output_examples.input: I want to compare a new model against published Aeon benchmark results.
output: Use the standard dataset split when available.
Report multiple metrics and compare against simple baselines.
Use statistical testing across datasets when claiming a meaningful improvement.

best_practices: Fit preprocessing and feature extraction on training data only to avoid leakage.
Start with fast baselines before using deep learning or large ensembles.
Use standard splits, fixed random seeds, and multiple metrics for reproducible comparisons.
anti_patterns: Do not treat shuffled tabular validation as a substitute for time-aware evaluation when order matters.
Do not choose DTW or deep learning by default without checking runtime and dataset size.
Do not report a single accuracy score without a baseline or uncertainty context.

faq.question: What is Aeon used for?
faq.answer: Aeon is used for machine learning on temporal and sequential data with scikit-learn compatible APIs.

faq.question: Does this skill run Aeon code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users run code in their own Python environment.

faq.question: Can it help with forecasting?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers baseline, statistical, regression-based, and deep learning forecasting options in Aeon.

faq.question: Does it support anomaly detection?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains point, subsequence, collection, and streaming anomaly detection choices.

faq.question: What data format does Aeon expect?
faq.answer: Many collection workflows expect samples, channels, and timepoints, often shaped as n_samples by n_channels by n_timepoints.

faq.question: Can I use Claude, Codex, or Claude Code with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
